text:
A coding tools company crossed multiple billions in ARR before hiring their first enterprise sales leader. Enterprise is now the majority of their revenue.

Most people read this as a PLG win. They're reading it wrong.

The interesting part isn't the absence of sales. It's the sequence. The product earned enterprise demand before anyone built a motion to capture it. By the time they hired a CRO-level revenue leader, Fortune 500 deployments were already happening through bottoms-up adoption. Sales became a capture function instead of a push function.

Compare that to what most "hybrid" PLG companies do: the free tier stalls near the conversion ceiling most free users never cross, so leadership bolts on an enterprise team, hands them a product that hasn't earned demand from a buying committee, then labels the resulting friction "sales-product misalignment."

The problem is sequencing.

My 12-24 month read: the companies that win enterprise in AI-native categories will be the ones whose products generate organic pull inside accounts before a rep shows up. The "hybrid PLG" label will split into two groups. One where sales captures existing demand. One where sales tries to manufacture it. The gap between those groups will be enormous.

For PMs now: stop optimizing for individual user activation. Start measuring account penetration. How many seats inside one logo, how fast, without a rep involved? That's the leading indicator of whether enterprise will work for you later.

What's the earliest signal you've seen that a product has earned enterprise demand, before sales gets involved?

#EnterpriseProduct #PLG #GTM #ProductManagement #B2BSaaS #ProductStrategy #SalesAlignment
